title_en: Family Law: Marriage, Divorce, and Inheritance
content_en:
The Hanbali school's approach to family law, as presented in Al-Mughni, is grounded in a strong commitment to Quranic injunctions, the authentic Sunnah, and the practices of the Companions. Ibn Qudamah's treatment of marriage, divorce, and inheritance is comprehensive, humane, and attentive to the rights of all parties.

Marriage in the Hanbali school requires: a specific offer and acceptance, the bride's guardian (wali) as a condition for the marriage's validity, two Muslim witnesses, and a mahr (dower). The wali requirement is based on the Prophet's saying: "There is no marriage without a guardian" (Abu Dawud, authenticated by Imam Ahmad). If the guardian refuses to marry off a woman who has a suitable match, the judge acts as her guardian. The mahr may be anything of monetary value, however small, and becomes the wife's exclusive property.

The Hanbali school permits a man to have up to four wives simultaneously, with the strict condition of equitable treatment in housing, spending, and nights. The school requires the husband to provide the wife with a suitable independent home, food, clothing, and maintenance proportional to his means. Failure to provide maintenance gives the wife the right to seek judicial dissolution of the marriage.

Divorce in the Hanbali school follows the Sunnah method carefully. A revocable divorce (talaq raj'i) pronounced once during a period of purity (not menstruation) may be revoked within the iddah (waiting period of three complete menstrual cycles) without a new contract. A second talaq is still revocable in the same session or another. After a third talaq, the divorce is final (bain) and the couple may not remarry until the woman has genuinely married and been divorced from another man.

Ibn Qudamah addresses at length the question of the triple talaq pronounced in one sitting (saying "you are divorced, divorced, divorced" at once) — a matter of major scholarly disagreement. Imam Ahmad's well-known position is that this counts as only one revocable divorce, based on the practice during the Prophet's time and the early caliphate. The majority position of the schools holds that it counts as three. This remains one of the most debated questions in Islamic family law, with significant practical consequences.

A woman may seek dissolution of the marriage through khul' — returning the mahr in exchange for her release. The Hanbali school holds that khul' requires the husband's agreement and constitutes an irrevocable separation (not a counted talaq). Alternatively, the judge may dissolve the marriage if the husband fails in his fundamental obligations.

Inheritance law (mawaris) is treated in detail based on the precise Quranic shares (fara'id) specified in Surah An-Nisa and the elaborating hadith. The Quran allocates fixed shares to spouses, parents, and children under specified conditions. The residue after fixed shares goes to male agnate relatives (asabah) in order of proximity. Daughters receive half the share of sons in most circumstances. The Prophet said: "Give the fixed shares to those entitled to them; what remains goes to the nearest male agnate" (Bukhari, Muslim).

Ibn Qudamah discusses the complex rules of inheritance in detail: the problem of simultaneous deaths, the rights of the child in the womb, the inheritance rights of those who cause death, and the different treatment of full, paternal, and maternal siblings. The precision of classical Hanbali inheritance calculations reflects centuries of scholarly engagement with the Quran's most mathematically detailed legal rulings.
